notes: MB embedded_mbid confirms Count Basie / 1993 / Columbia+Legacy. albumDNA is verbatim from the Count Basie Orchestra wiki page and describes the band (founded 1935, recording 1936, Basie died 1984, now under Scotty Barnhart) -- all consistent with the wiki extract. All 5 keyTracks present in MB tracklist. The stored wikipediaUrl points to the Count Basie Orchestra page (the album's performing group), not a dedicated album page; it is about our artist's band, so not a wrong-entity collision.

MB embedded_mbid and wiki both about this exact album (James Blood Ulmer / 1993 / DIW / Indigo String Quartet / harmolodics). albumDNA verbatim from the album wiki page. All 5 keyTracks match the MB tracklist.

MB embedded_mbid confirms the album identity: Julius Hemphill & Abdul Wadud / 1993 / Music & Arts; all 5 keyTracks match the MB tracklist. No wikipedia evidence. The substantive recording-venue and exact-date claims (live at the Oakland Museum, November 13-14 1992) and the 'live album' characterization cannot be checked against MB, which carries no venue/date detail. No contradiction found -- identity is solid, only those particulars are unverifiable.

MB embedded_mbid confirms Benny Goodman / 1993 / Star Line Productions. The three tracks named in the DNA and all 5 keyTracks are present in the MB tracklist. Remaining DNA is generic editorial era-framing (not a factual claim). Stored wikipediaUrl is the Benny Goodman artist page, which is about our artist.

wrongClaims.ourClaim: He came to fame as a teen pop singer in the late 1990s, establishing himself as a star among preteen and teenage audiences during the first years of the 2000s, with his four studio albums.
wrongClaims.problem: Contaminating biographical text about Aaron Carter (a teen-pop singer) grafted onto a Betty Carter album blurb. Our entity is Betty Carter, a female jazz vocalist (MB credits 'Betty Carter' with Geri Allen / Dave Holland / Jack DeJohnette); the masculine pronoun and the teen-pop-stardom claim do not belong to her.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Aaron Charles Carter (December 7, 1987 - November 5, 2022) was an American singer and rapper. He rose to fame as a teen pop singer in the late 1990s, establishing himself as a star among preteen and teenage audiences during the early 2000s, with his four studio albums.

notes: Album identity IS correct: MB embedded_mbid confirms Betty Carter / 1994 / Jazz Door (bootleg) with Geri Allen, Dave Holland, Jack DeJohnette; all 6 keyTracks match the MB tracklist. The first two DNA sentences correctly describe Betty Carter. The disambiguation-collision bug shows up twice here: (1) the final DNA sentence is lifted verbatim from the Aaron Carter wiki page, and (2) the stored wikipediaUrl is https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aaron_Carter -- a wholly different, unrelated person. Classified factual_error (not entity_mismatch) because only the trailing sentence belongs to another entity; the album's factual skeleton is genuinely Betty Carter's. Repair: delete the Aaron Carter sentence and fix the wiki link.

MB embedded_mbid confirms Herbie Hancock / 1994 / Warner Archives, with releaseGroup secondaryType 'Compilation' -- consistent with a 'Complete ... Recordings' anthology. All 5 keyTracks match the MB tracklist. Remaining DNA (jazz fusion / jazz-funk, era-framing) is editorial genre description. No wikipedia evidence; ours.wikipediaUrl is null.

MB search match (similarity 0.75) and wiki both about this album (Jan Garbarek & The Hilliard Ensemble / 1994 / ECM, recorded at Propstei St. Gerold, Austria, Sept 1993). albumDNA's factual core is verbatim from the album wiki page. All 4 keyTracks match the MB tracklist (case-insensitive; 'Parce Mihi Domine', 'Beata Viscera', 'Credo', 'Sanctus' all present). Caveat: the DNA's 'Became ECM's best-selling album ever' is stronger than the wiki's 'one of the most successful releases on the ECM label, achieving sales of more than 1.5 million' -- an editorial superlative the evidence neither confirms nor contradicts; not flagged as factual_error (absence is not contradiction).

MB embedded_mbid and wiki both about this album (Bill Evans / orchestra / Claus Ogerman, recorded Feb 1974, originally on German MPS). albumDNA verbatim from the album wiki page. ours.year=1994 is the MPS/Verve CD reissue (wiki: 'released on compact disc by Verve Records in 1994'; MB shows 1994 MPS) -- consistent with the DNA's 'recorded in February 1974 and originally released on the German MPS label'. keyTracks are the five subsections of the two-movement work; MB formats them as '1st/2nd Movement (Moderato/Largo...): a/b/c)' while ours uses 'Symbiosis: Ia/IIa...' -- same movements, the descriptive tempo markings match (e.g. 'Largo - Andante - Maestoso - Largo'), so treated as ok with a formatting difference, not phantom tracks.

wrongClaims.problem: None of the six listed keyTracks appears on this release. The MB tracklist for this Squirrel Thing Recordings 'Tidal Wave' compilation is a different selection of Fletcher Henderson sides.
wrongClaims.evidenceQuote: Hocus Pocus / Hocus Pocus (alternative version) / Phantom Phantasy / Harlem Madness / Tidal Wave / Limehouse Blues / Shanghai Shuffle / Big John's Special / Happy as the Day Is Long / Tidal Wave / Down South Camp Meeting / Wrappin' It Up / Memphis Blues / Wild Party / Rug Cutter's Swing / Hotter Than 'Ell / Liza (All the Clouds'll Roll Away) / Christopher Columbus / Grand Terrace Swing (Big Chief de Sota) / Blue Lou / Stealin' Apples

notes: Right entity: MB embedded_mbid confirms Fletcher Henderson / 1994 / Squirrel Thing Recordings (an early-jazz compilation of his 1930s sides). The albumDNA prose itself is consistent with MB -- it names 'Hocus Pocus' and 'Hocus Pocus (alternative version)', which are MB tracks 1-2. But the keyTracks field lists six songs, NONE of which are on this release per the MB tracklist, so each is a false 'on this album' claim -> factual_error (keyTracks mismatch caps severity at factual_error). ours.wikipediaUrl is null; the opensearch result landed on the 'Tidal wave' disambiguation page (not an album), discarded.

No contradiction; album identity is solid.

MB embedded_mbid confirms Charles Gayle / 1995 / Les Disques Victo; all 5 keyTracks match the MB tracklist exactly (Innocent / Pastures Colors / Eden Lost / Good Shepherd / Childs Love). MB embedded_mbid confirms Andy Bey / 1996 / Evidence; all 5 keyTracks match the MB tracklist (Someone to Watch Over Me / You'd Be So Nice to Come Home To / I Let a Song Go Out of My Heart / In a Sentimental Mood / Willow Weep for Me). MB search match (similarity 1.0); release title matches exactly and Benny Goodman is among the credited artists (alongside Mozart, Bartok, Gershwin, Toscanini, Szigeti, Earl Wild) / 1996 / Magic Talent. All 5 keyTracks match the MB tracklist (Mozart Clarinet Quintet K581 movements, Bartok 'Contrasts'). Caveat for a repair-writer: the DNA frames this as Goodman 'working in a swing vein', but the repertoire evidenced by the tracklist is classical (Mozart Clarinet Quintet, Bartok Contrasts, Gershwin Rhapsody in Blue) -- a boilerplate genre/era mischaracterization. Treated as editorial framing rather than a flagged factual claim per the spec; the factual fields (title/artist/label/year/tracks) all check. No wikipedia evidence.
